facts.text: Granville is called the "Colored Slate Capital of the World" and quarries produce slate in colors like green, gray, purple, and red.
The first slate deposits were found in 1839 and the first quarries opened around 1853, shaping the town’s industrial heritage.
Granville’s historic Slate Valley Museum and Pember Library and Museum showcase the town’s slate legacy and local history.
The village hosts an annual Lighted Tractor Parade that celebrates its culture and draws visitors from across the region.
Granville lies on the eastern border of Washington County, abutting Vermont’s Rutland County, and is part of the Glens Falls Metropolitan Statistical Area.
The Dayton-Williams House, South Granville Congregational Church, and Town-Hollister Farm are all listed on the National Register of Historic Places.
